attachment.content:
- plan-task: Plan-and-converge loop for non-trivial tasks with adversarial review. Use when the user explicitly sends you to do, implement, add, build, refactor, port, wire-up, check, verify, audit, inspect, or otherwise execute a non-trivial task. Forms a concrete execution plan (including test coverage — new tests + regressions to run), spawns an adversarial subagent to challenge the plan, iterates until both converge on a shared TLDR plan, reports only the TLDR back, waits for user approval, then executes and runs regressions. Do NOT invoke for trivial edits the user already specified line-by-line, single-file renames, one-off questions, or pure explanations.
- recover-context: When you have missing context from a previous compacted session, search the pre-compaction transcript to fill the gaps instead of asking the user to repeat themselves.
- extract-file-requirements: Extract user requirements from the user's prompts and persist them into `requirements-{filename}.md` sidecar files next to each relevant source file, AND enforce those sidecars on every subsequent edit. Use this skill whenever the user's prompt contains constraints, rules, musts, must-nots, invariants, behavioral specs, acceptance criteria, or design decisions that relate to specific files in the codebase — even when the user does not explicitly say "save requirements". Also use it when the user amends, relaxes, or overrides a previously stated requirement so the sidecar stays in sync. ALSO use this skill BEFORE editing, writing, or refactoring any file in the codebase: read its sidecar (if any) and the sidecars of files it tightly couples to, and refuse to introduce changes that violate a recorded requirement without explicit user approval. Skill ONLY records things the user literally said in their prompts — never requirements inferred from existing code, tests, docs, or the assistant's own chain of thought.
- mine-previous-sessions: Search previous sessions for user-stated requirements, decisions, and preferences. Use when you're unsure about a requirement, a decision seems ambiguous, or you need more context on what the user explicitly asked for. Historical user messages are the ground truth for intent.
- investigate-issue: Root-cause investigation loop with adversarial review. Use ONLY when the user explicitly sends you to investigate an issue, bug, mismatch, unexpected behavior, divergence, regression, parity break, or "why is X happening". Drills to root cause (not symptoms), forms a fix plan including test coverage (regression test that catches this bug + regressions to run), spawns an adversarial subagent to challenge ALL THREE (cause + fix + tests), iterates until convergence, then reports only the TLDR back. Do NOT invoke for general questions, code explanations, feature requests, or trivial fixes the user already knows the cause of.
- ccc: This skill should be used when code search, file/directory summary lookup, or concept-guide lookup is needed (whether explicitly requested or as part of completing a task), when indexing the codebase after changes, or when the user asks about ccc, cocoindex-code, or the codebase index. Trigger phrases include 'search the codebase', 'find code related to', 'describe this file', 'read the concept guide', 'update the index', 'ccc', 'cocoindex-code'.
- project-structure: Explains the layout and architecture of the "Better Claude" monorepo at /Users/dev/better-claude — a web UI for Claude Code built around a persistent manager Claude Code session that delegates work to worker Claude Code sessions (Python FastAPI backend + React/TypeScript frontend). Use this whenever you need this repo's structure, where a specific file or concept lives, how to run the project, how the backend and frontend relate, how the manager delegates to workers, how the `delegate` MCP tool works, or any "what is this repo" / "where does X live" / "how does X work here" style question. Also use as an orientation pass before making non-trivial edits in this repo. Answers with progressive disclosure — one-liner first, details only when the user's question demands them. YOU HAVE TO KEEP THIS UPDATED WHEN YOU NOTICE A CHANGE IN MATERIAL STUFF.
- project: Project Structure — Better Claude
- trace: Trace Inspector — Better Claude Orchestration
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.args: how to run / launch the app

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/dev/better-claude/.claude/skills/project-structure

# Better Claude — project structure

Use this skill to orient yourself (and the user) to the Better Claude repo. The repo is a web UI for Claude Code with **three selectable orchestration modes**: the default **manager / worker coordinator** (one persistent "manager" Claude Code session per UI session delegates real work to **worker Better Claude sessions** via a custom `delegate` MCP tool; per-(caller, worker) delegate forks are themselves embedded BC sessions with `kind="delegate_fork"`), the simpler **native** mode (a single persistent Claude Code session per UI session, no manager, no workers, no MCP tool, no prompt wrapping), and **supervisor** mode (one user-facing supervisor session paired 1:1 with an internal `kind="supervisor_worker"` BC session embedded under it; user prompts go to the worker via `_run_turn`, and an inline `Stop` hook on the worker calls back into the backend, which asks the supervisor session for a verdict — `CONTINUE` / `FIX` / `DONE` — and either lets the worker stop or feeds the supervisor's instructions back as additional input, capped at 3 verdicts per turn). The mode is a per-session setting that the user picks from an "Orchestration" selector in the sidebar; manager mode shows a Workers panel beneath the selector for managing the per-cwd worker registry. Backend is Python + FastAPI in `backend/`, frontend is React + TypeScript (Vite) in `frontend/`.

## How to respond — match the question's depth

Claude's attention and the user's patience are both limited. Answer at the depth the question actually demands. A casual "what's this repo?" deserves one sentence; a focused "walk me through how delegation works" deserves real detail. If you preemptively dump every level, the answer the user actually wanted gets buried and you spend context you'll want later.

Use the ladder below as a routing table. Only read a reference file when the question has clearly reached it.

1. **"What is this repo?"** → the one-liner at the top of this file. Stop there.
2. **"How do I run it?"** → the *Running the project* section below.
3. **"Where does X live?" / "What's the layout?"** → the *Directory tree* section below.
4. **"How does the manager/worker thing work?" / "How does `delegate` work?" / "How does a user prompt become a response?" / "How do events reach the frontend?"** → read `references/architecture.md`. That file explains the manager coordinator, the `delegate` MCP round-trip, how the manager reads worker jsonl files, the WebSocket event stream, and cancellation.
5. **"Tell me about `<specific module>`"** → read `references/modules.md` for the pointer, then read the file itself rather than paraphrasing from memory.
6. **"How do I test / lint / what are the conventions?"** → read `references/conventions.md`.

If the question is clearly about one specific file, just read that file directly — don't re-explain the whole project first.

## Running the project

```
# Both at once (kills previous, restarts fresh)
./run.sh

# Or individually:
# Backend (port 8000)
cd backend && source .venv/bin/activate && uvicorn main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

# Frontend (port 5173)
cd frontend && npm run dev

# CLI driver for the orchestration layer (no browser)
cd backend && source .venv/bin/activate
python cli.py                         # interactive REPL
python cli.py -p "do the thing"       # one-shot
python cli.py --json -p "..."         # jsonl output for scripting
# Auto-detects a running backend on :8000; falls back to in-process uvicorn.
```

Both servers are also configured in `.claude/launch.json` for Claude Code's preview system.

## Directory tree

```
backend/
  main.py               FastAPI app. /ws/chat WebSocket, REST endpoints for
                        sessions, files, git, traces.
  orchestrator.py       ManagerCoordinator class — shared per-session
                        infra only (locks, WS callbacks, in-flight refs,
                        cancellation events, the `_run_turn` wrapper that
                        does trace/persist/WS fan-out, and shared helpers
                        `_compute_jsonl_path` / `_count_jsonl_lines` /
                        `_extract_output_text`). `handle_prompt` dispatches
                        by session.orchestration_mode through `orchs.get_handler(mode)`
                        — no per-mode branches in this file. Mode handlers
                        live under `backend/orchs/<mode>/`. Manager-only
                        methods (run_delegation, _resolve_approval, etc.)
                        remain on the class as thin proxies that import
                        + forward to free functions in `orchs.manager._delegation`
                        / `_approval` / `_rewind`. After each turn's
                        completion event, fire-and-forgets
                        `rearranger.trigger_final(sid)` when bound.
  orchs/                Per-orchestration-mode packages.
                        - `orchs/__init__.py` — `get_handler(mode)` registry.
                        - `orchs/native/__init__.py` — `handle_turn` (thin
                          wrapper that calls coordinator._run_turn with
                          mode="native", no MCP wrapping).
                        - `orchs/manager/__init__.py` — `handle_turn` (wraps
                          prompt with BOOTSTRAP_PROMPT + known_workers,
                          forwards to coordinator._run_turn with
                          mode="manager"); lazy-exports `run_delegation`
                          and `resolve_approval` via PEP 562 `__getattr__`
                          (avoids circular import with session_store).
                        - `orchs/manager/bootstrap.py` — BOOTSTRAP_PROMPT
                          + format_known_workers + build_wrapped_prompt.
                        - `orchs/manager/_delegation.py` — run_delegation,
                          run_delegation_locked, lock_for_pair,
                          delegate_error_payload (the delegate round-trip
                          + per-pair fork mint/resume).
                        - `orchs/manager/_approval.py` — resolve_approval,
                          await_fresh_worker_approval, spawn_approved_worker,
                          init_worker_claude_session.
                        - `orchs/manager/_rewind.py` — rewind_workers_for_turn,
                          first_user_uuid_in_panel.
                        - `orchs/manager/worker_store.py` — moved from
                          backend/. Per-cwd worker registry + per-pair fork
                          mapping at ~/.better-claude/workers/<encoded-cwd>.json.
                        - `orchs/manager/pending_approvals.py` — moved from
                          backend/. Disk-backed approval store for
                          fresh-worker delegate calls.
                        - `orchs/supervisor/__init__.py` — `handle_turn`
                          finds-or-creates the paired `kind=
                          "supervisor_worker"` BC session and runs the
                          worker via `_run_turn` with `mode="native"` +
                          `supervised=True`. The worker's discovered
                          claude_sid is persisted to the WORKER BC's
                          `native_claude_session_id` (NOT the
                          supervisor's) so multi-turn supervisor
                          sessions can accumulate context.
                        - `orchs/supervisor/_verdict.py` — backend
                          handler for the supervised worker's `Stop`
                          hook. Spawns `claude -p --resume
                          <supervisor_claude_session_id>` (or fresh
                          on first call) to ask the supervisor for
                          CONTINUE/FIX/DONE; capped at
                          MAX_VERDICTS_PER_TURN=3 per (sup, worker)
                          pair. Cap-hit and crash paths broadcast a
                          `supervisor_event` WS frame so failures are
                          surface-able. Mints + persists the
                          supervisor's claude_sid on first call.
  provider.py           Provider ABC — `start_run`, `cancel_run`, `run_headless`,
                        `rewind`, `recover_in_flight`, `prune_old_runs`,
                        `cancel_all`, `active_runs`, `runs_for_session`,
                        `build_env`, `available_models`. Registry in
                        `_resolve_class(kind)` maps "claude"/"gemini" to
                        concrete Provider subclasses. `active_provider()`
                        and `get_provider(id)` return cached instances.
  provider_claude.py    ClaudeProvider — spawns `runner.py` detached, tails
                        Claude's session jsonl via `ClaudeJsonlTailer`.
                        Also hosts `_enrich_claude_line`, `_SubagentRegistry`,
                        `RunState`, and shared helpers for crash recovery.
  provider_gemini.py    GeminiProvider — spawns `runner_gemini.py` detached,
                        tails the runner's `session_events.jsonl` (normalized
                        to Claude jsonl shape by the runner). Subscription-only.
                        No rewind support. Manager mode not yet implemented.
  runner.py             Detached per-run executable for Claude. Uses
                        `claude_agent_sdk` to drive the claude CLI. Writes
                        `state.json`, `complete.json`. One runner = one run.
  runner_gemini.py      Detached per-run executable for Gemini. Spawns
                        `gemini -p <prompt> -o stream-json`, parses events,
                        normalizes to Claude jsonl format, writes to
                        `session_events.jsonl`. Subscription-only.
  models.py             Dynamic model discovery scoped to active provider.
                        Claude subscription → static aliases. Gemini → static
                        model list. API key mode → GET {base_url}/v1/models.
                        All modes merge with provider's custom_models.
  config_store.py       Provider CRUD in ~/.better-claude/config.json. Records
                        carry `kind` ("claude"/"gemini"), `mode`, `base_url`,
                        `config_dir`, `custom_models`, `default_model`. API keys
                        in macOS Keychain. `apply_env_vars()` sets os.environ
                        from the active provider's settings.
  session_store.py      Tree-shape JSON persistence in ~/.better-claude/sessions/
                        (schema v2). One file per ROOT session; forks are
                        embedded inside the root's `forks: [Session, ...]`
                        array (recursive — forks can nest). Top-level
                        functions: get_session, get_root_tree,
                        write_session_full (root only), fork_session,
                        delete_session, list_sessions (roots only),
                        iter_all_sessions (root + every embedded fork,
                        depth-first). In-memory `_fork_index: {fork_id:
                        root_id}` resolves any sid back to its root file
                        in O(1); warmed on first access. Records carry
                        orchestration_mode, manager/native_claude_session_id,
                        parent_session_id, fork_point_seq (parent's last
                        seq at fork time — drives the split-pane render),
                        fork_closed (locks a fork from receiving prompts),
                        plus rearranger fields. Workers are NOT stored on
                        session records (see worker_store.py). Legacy v1
                        fork files (separate top-level files with parent_session_id
                        set) raise on read with "wipe ~/.better-claude/sessions/."
  session_manager.py    Tree-aware single-writer over session_store. Cache
                        is keyed per ROOT (`_roots[root_id]`); any sid
                        resolves to its root via `_node_root_id`; locks
                        are per-root-tree (siblings serialize). Public
                        API: get(sid), get_root_tree(sid), get_ref(sid),
                        list() (sidebar summary, roots only), iter_all()
                        (every node), batch(sid), create(), fork(parent),
                        delete(sid), plus typed mutators (set_selectors,
                        set_claude_sid, set_fork_closed, append_user_msg,
                        ...). Listener fan-out via `add_listener` —
                        session_ws_broadcaster turns those into WS frames.
                        Every other backend module reads/writes through
                        this; direct session_store calls are a code smell.
  (worker_store + pending_approvals moved into orchs/manager/ — see above.)
                        Pruned by mtime > 7d at startup.
  rearranger.py         Experimental: side Claude CLI coordinator that emits
                        a hierarchical JSON intent tree per opt-in UI
                        session. Runs `claude -p --output-format json
                        --permission-mode bypassPermissions` as a plain
                        asyncio subprocess (NOT via ProviderBridge). Per-
                        session asyncio ticker + per-session lock; fires on
                        a ~20s tick AND on every turn_complete/turn_stopped.
                        Lazy global bootstrap session + linear fork chain
                        per UI session (first run for a session forks off
                        the bootstrap, each subsequent run forks off the
                        prior per-session sid). Broadcasts
                        `rearranger_updated` / `rearranger_state` WS events
                        via its own per-session callback registry (distinct
                        from ManagerCoordinator's turn-scoped one).
  rearranger_prompt.py  BOOTSTRAP_PROMPT + build_diff_prompt() for the
                        rearranger — trims session messages to role +
                        content and inlines only the new-message delta
                        each run, so each CLI call stays tiny.
  rearranger_state.py   Tiny persistence for the global rearranger
                        bootstrap sid at ~/.better-claude/rearranger_state.json.
  project_store.py      JSON file persistence at ~/.better-claude/projects.json
                        for the sidebar "Projects" picker. On first read
                        (no file yet) seeds the list from the distinct
                        cwds of existing sessions. REST: /api/projects
                        (list/add/delete) and /api/projects/touch (bump
                        last_used). Also auto-added on first-turn of any
                        session whose cwd isn't already in the list.
  file_browser.py       File tree & git utilities for the right-panel file viewer.
  trace_collector.py    Captures trace events during a turn.
  trace_cli.py          CLI tool for inspecting captured traces.
  cli.py                CLI driver for the orchestration layer. REPL or
                        one-shot (-p). Auto-detects a backend on :8000 —
                        if reachable, connects as a websocket client to
                        /ws/chat; otherwise starts uvicorn in-process on a
                        free port and drives ManagerCoordinator directly
                        (sets BETTER_CLAUDE_BACKEND_URL so the manager
                        MCP's /api/internal/delegate callback round-trips
                        to the same coordinator instance). Pretty renderer
                        by default, --json for raw jsonl. Uses a "cli-default"
                        session keyed by cwd.
  requirements.txt

frontend/src/
  App.tsx               Root: 3-panel layout (sidebar, chat, file viewer).
                        Holds the split-fork state: `currentTree` (root +
                        embedded forks from useSession) and `focusedForkId`
                        (which pane sends/drafts target). The local
                        `currentSession` alias = focused node, so the
                        80+ existing references to currentSession keep
                        working without a sweeping rename.
  main.tsx              Vite entry
  components/           Chat (renders ForkSplitView when tree.forks
                        non-empty, otherwise the linear list),
                        MessageBubble (~585, largest), InputArea (with
                        Send + optional Fork buttons), ForkSplitView
                        (shared messages above fork_point_seq + N+1
                        side-by-side panes below; focus radio top-right,
                        close button bottom-right of each pane),
                        SessionList, FileTree, FileViewer, ToolCall,
                        ThinkingBlock, JsonNode, TokenUsage,
                        ModelSelector, OrchestrationSelector, TraceViewer
  hooks/                useWebSocket.ts (multi-session subscribe via
                        additionalAppSessionIds), useSession.ts
                        (tree-aware: currentSession holds the whole
                        root tree; reducers walk the tree by id via
                        updateNodeById), useTraces.ts
  styles/globals.css    Dark theme, accent #7b68ee. Includes
                        `.fork-split` / `.fork-pane*` styles for the
                        side-by-side fork view.
  types.ts              Shared TypeScript types (Session.forks,
                        Session.fork_point_seq, Session.fork_closed,
                        WorkerPanel, etc.)
  threadColors.ts       Per-worker color assignment (`buildThreadColorMap`). Named
                        historically; it now colors worker panels.
```

Session data lives outside the repo in `~/.better-claude/sessions/` (JSON files — one per ROOT session; forks are embedded in their root's `forks` array, never separate files), with images under `sessions/images/{session_id}/`. Schema v2; v1 fork files (separate top-level files with `parent_session_id` set) raise on read — wipe `~/.better-claude/sessions/` to start fresh. The sidebar "Projects" picker list is stored at `~/.better-claude/projects.json` (see `project_store.py`). Each CLI run (manager or worker) writes its streaming events to `~/.better-claude/runs/{run_id}/events.jsonl`, with `input.json`, `pid`, `stdout.log`, `stderr.log`, and (on completion) `complete.json` alongside; the backend tails `events.jsonl` to get a live stream, and the runner directory is the durable record that outlives backend restarts. Worker Claude session jsonl files (what the manager reads back to see what each worker did) live under `~/.claude/projects/{encoded-cwd}/{session_id}.jsonl` — those are the *real* Claude Code session files, distinct from our per-run events.jsonl.

## Keeping this skill current

This skill is a condensed mental model of Better Claude. Future Claude sessions will trust it — which is a speedup when it's accurate and actively harmful when it's stale, because confident wrong answers spread downstream. (A previous version of this file described a router/threads/distill architecture that was already replaced by the manager/worker coordinator; Claude sessions reading it confidently explained phases and files that no longer existed. A stale skill is worse than no skill at all.)

**If you're making a material change to the project, update this skill in the same turn as part of the change — don't defer it.** A change is "material" if a future Claude reading this skill would, as a result, give a wrong or misleading answer about the project. Rough guide:

- **Add, remove, or rename a backend module or a notable frontend component** → update the `Directory tree` above and the pointers in `references/modules.md`.
- **Change how the manager/worker coordinator works** — restructure `ManagerCoordinator`, change the `delegate` MCP tool's schema or semantics, change the `BOOTSTRAP_PROMPT`, change how the manager reads worker jsonl files, change worker persistence/resume, or replace the architecture wholesale → update `references/architecture.md`, and if the core framing moves, re-frame the top of that file.
- **Add or remove a WebSocket event type** (`manager_*`, `worker_*`, `turn_*`, etc.) → update the events list in `references/architecture.md`.
- **Break a stated convention** — e.g., introducing an ORM, adding a state-management library, moving the `~/.better-claude/` storage layout, adding a linter/formatter config — → update `references/conventions.md` and reconsider whether the convention still holds at all.
- **Change how to run the project** (new prerequisite, env var, port, script) → update the `Running the project` block above.

Don't bother updating for: bug fixes, small refactors inside one file, lint/style cleanups, new tests, dependency bumps, or anything that doesn't change the answer to a question this skill would be asked. The bar is *would a future Claude, reading this skill, now be wrong about the project?* — if no, leave it alone.

When in doubt, err on the side of a small update: a one-line tweak to a file description or a pointer is cheap, and the compounding cost of letting the skill rot is high.


ARGUMENTS: how to run / launch the app
Launching via `./run.sh` (starts backend on :8000 and frontend on :5173).

content.content:
#!/bin/bash

DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"

# Kill previous processes by port
echo "Killing previous processes..."
lsof -ti :8000 | xargs kill -9 2>/dev/null
lsof -ti :5173 | xargs kill -9 2>/dev/null
sleep 1

# Start backend
echo "Starting backend..."
(cd "$DIR/backend" && source .venv/bin/activate && uvicorn main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000) &
BACKEND_PID=$!

# Start frontend
echo "Starting frontend..."
(cd "$DIR/frontend" && npm run dev) &
FRONTEND_PID=$!

echo "Backend PID: $BACKEND_PID"
echo "Frontend PID: $FRONTEND_PID"
echo "Press Ctrl+C to stop both."

trap "kill $BACKEND_PID $FRONTEND_PID 2>/dev/null; exit" SIGINT SIGTERM
wait

toolUseResult.stdout:
#!/bin/bash

DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"

# Kill previous processes by port
echo "Killing previous processes..."
lsof -ti :8000 | xargs kill -9 2>/dev/null
lsof -ti :5173 | xargs kill -9 2>/dev/null
sleep 1

# Start backend
echo "Starting backend..."
(cd "$DIR/backend" && source .venv/bin/activate && uvicorn main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000) &
BACKEND_PID=$!

# Start frontend
echo "Starting frontend..."
(cd "$DIR/frontend" && npm run dev) &
FRONTEND_PID=$!

echo "Backend PID: $BACKEND_PID"
echo "Frontend PID: $FRONTEND_PID"
echo "Press Ctrl+C to stop both."

trap "kill $BACKEND_PID $FRONTEND_PID 2>/dev/null; exit" SIGINT SIGTERM
wait
